返回本批次

人工智能突然对开源开发人员变得更加有用

AI has suddenly become more useful to open-source developers

www.zdnet.com 作者 CrankyBear 分数 53 评论 46 2026/04/01 22:07
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. imaginima/ iStock / Getty Images Plus 来自 Getty Images 关注 ZDNET：将我们添加为 Google 上的首选来源。ZDNET 的关键要点顶级开源维护者发现 AI 突然变得更加有用。仍然有法律和“AI 溢出”问题需要克服。到年底，AI 编程工具应该更加可靠。随着开源软件运行几乎所有内容，您可能会认为多个开发人员在企业赞助商的帮助下维护大多数重要程序。你错了。软件供应链公司 Anchore 的安全副总裁 Josh Bressers 去年指出，绝大多数开源项目（1180 万个程序中的 700 万个）只有一个维护者。您可能认为这些程序晦涩难懂或不再使用。你也错了。另外：我用来交付真实、可靠产品的 7 种人工智能编码技术 - fastBressers 仔细研究了 JavaScript NPM 生态系统，发现在每月下载超过 100 万次的项目中，“下载最多的 13,000 个 NPM 包中约有一半是由一个人[维护]的。”哦！换句话说，数以千计的重要项目只需一场车祸或心脏病就可能被毁掉。这不太好。人工智能工具最近在编码方面变得更好了，我们能做些什么呢？你不可能挥动魔杖就奇迹般地找到数千名随时待命的专家维护人员。相反，一些著名的开源维护者一直在考虑使用人工智能来保持遗留代码库的活力或使其更易于维护。这是可能的，因为无论你相信与否，人工智能编码工具最近在编码方面变得更加出色。这不是我的观点。在我最好的时候，我是一个不错的程序员。不，这是 Linux 稳定内核的维护者 Greg Kroah-Hartman 的观点。Kroah-Hartman 和我最近在阿姆斯特丹的 KubeCon Europe 聚会。他告诉我，“几个月前，我们收到了所谓的‘AI slop’，即人工智能生成的安全报告，这些报告显然是错误的或质量低下的。”另外：为什么人工智能对开源软件来说既是诅咒又是祝福——根据开发人员的说法然后，奇妙的事情发生了。 “一个月前，”他继续说道，“世界发生了变化。现在我们有了真实的报告。所有开源项目都有用人工智能制作的真实报告，但它们很好，而且是真实的。所有开源安全团队现在都在解决这个问题。”发生了什么？ Kroah-Hartman 耸耸肩：“我们不知道。似乎没有人知道为什么。要么有更多的工具变得更好，要么人们开始说，‘嘿，让我们开始看看这个。’”现在，这并不意味着 Anthropic Claude 将很快取代 Linus Torvalds，甚至是你们公司的中级程序员。不过，它的真正含义是，如果使用得当——这里没有振动编码——人工智能可以帮助清理旧的但仍在使用的代码；维护废弃的程序；另外：人工智能的过度销售——以及如何抵制它例如，Verizon 开源高级总监 Dirk Hondhel 在 LinkedIn 上发帖称，虽然人工智能编码工具尚未准备好维护代码，但他相信很快就会准备好。 “今天这几乎是可能的。按照过去几个季度这些工具的改进速度，我相信今年某个时候有可能取得可接受的结果。” 他并不是唯一一个这样的人。 Ruby 项目维护者 Stan Lo (st0012) 写道，人工智能已经在文档主题、重构和调试方面帮助了他，他明确想知道人工智能工具是否会“帮助恢复未维护的项目”并“培养新一代贡献者，甚至维护者”。事实上，已经有一个人工智能项目，即遗留应用系统的自主翻译（ATLAS），可以帮助开发人员实现现代编程语言的遗留代码库的现代化。我们预计很快就会看到其他此类人工智能工具的出现。有很多过时但仍在使用的代码可以使用现代刷新。律师们将大干一场 在开香槟之前，让我们考虑几个主要问题。首先，如果我们可以用人工智能改进开源代码，那么什么才能阻止有人复制和重写现有代码，然后将其置于专有许可之下呢？律师们将对此进行深入研究。哦，等等！ ——他们很快就会这样做：Dan Blanchard，一个名为 chardet 的重要 Python 库的维护者，刚刚在 MIT 许可证下发布了该程序的最新“洁净室”版本，取代了其 GNU 宽通用公共许可证 (LGPL)。他所说的“洁净室”是指他使用 Anthropic 的 Claude 完全重写了图书馆。克劳德现在被列为项目贡献者。自称是该项目原始开发者的马克·皮尔格里姆 (Mark Pilgrim) 并不高兴。 Pilgrim 说：“[维护者]声称这是‘完全重写’是无关紧要的，因为他们充分接触了原始许可的代码。在混合中添加一个花哨的代码生成器并不会“另外：人工智能在发现隐藏的软件错误方面变得越来越擅长——即使是在几十年前的代码中。然而，布兰查德声称“chardet 7 不是早期版本的衍生品。”我有没有提到使用人工智能修改或克隆开源代码最终会上法庭？还有另一个问题：虽然看起来人工智能在修复代码问题方面比以前更有用，但仍然有很多人工智能的废品和开源项目几乎每个开源项目的维护者都深陷其中。在某些情况下，人工智能溢出已经被证明是非常有害的，例如，Jazzband 的首席维护者 Python 软件基金会的 Jannis Leidel 关闭了该程序，因为“人工智能生成的垃圾邮件和问题”淹没了该项目。一位谨慎的人工智能用户警告说，虽然人工智能可以快速生成代码，但其结果可能“难以维护”。他将人工智能视为一种提高生产力的工具，但它并不能取代在出现问题时实际了解程序中发生的情况的需要。而且，我向你保证，事情将会崩溃。另外：Claude Code 的新自动模式如何防止人工智能编码灾难 - 而不会减慢你的速度Linux 基金会的安全组织、Alpha-Omega 项目和开源安全基金会。 (OpenSSF) 正在通过免费向维护人员提供人工智能工具来解决这个问题，Kroah-Hartman 表示，“OpenSSF 拥有支持众多项目所需的活跃资源，这将帮助这些过度劳累的维护人员对他们目前收到的越来越多的人工智能生成的安全报告进行分类和处理。”和谐。

Original Summary

  1. imaginima/ iStock / Getty Images Plus via Getty ImagesFollow ZDNET: Add us as a preferred source on Google.ZDNET's key takeawaysTop open-source maintainers find that AI has suddenly become much more useful.There are still legal and 'AI slop' problems to overcome.By year's end, AI programming tools should be much more reliable.With open-source software running pretty much everything, you might think that multiple developers maintain most of the important programs with help from corporate sponsors. You'd be wrong.As Josh Bressers, VP of security at software supply-chain company Anchore, pointed out last year, the vast majority of open-source projects, 7 million out of 11.8 million programs, have only a single maintainer. You might think that those programs are obscure or no longer used. You'd be wrong about that, too. Also: 7 AI coding techniques I use to ship real, reliable products - fastBressers looked closely at the JavaScript NPM ecosystem and found that, among the projects downloaded over a million times a month, "about half of the 13,000 most downloaded NPM packages are [maintained by] one person."Ow! To think of it another way, thousands of vital programs are one car accident or heart attack away from being knocked out. That is not good.AI tools have recently become much better at codingWhat can we do about it? You can't wave a magic wand and miraculously find thousands of ready-to-go expert maintainers. Instead, several prominent open-source maintainers have been considering using AI to keep legacy codebases alive or to make them easier to maintain.That's possible because, believe it or not, AI coding tools have recently become much better at coding. That's not my opinion. At my best, I was an OK programmer. No, that's the opinion of Greg Kroah-Hartman, maintainer of the Linux stable kernel.Kroah-Hartman and I got together at KubeCon Europe in Amsterdam recently. He told me, "Months ago, we were getting what we called 'AI slop,' AI-generated security reports that were obviously wrong or low quality." Also: Why AI is both a curse and a blessing to open-source software - according to developersThen, something wonderful happened. "A month ago," he continued, "the world switched. Now we have real reports. All open-source projects have real reports that are made with AI, but they're good, and they're real. All open source security teams are hitting this right now."What happened? Kroah-Hartman shrugged: "We don't know. Nobody seems to know why. Either a lot more tools got a lot better, or people started going, 'Hey, let's start looking at this.'"Now that doesn't mean that Anthropic Claude is going to replace Linus Torvalds anytime soon, or even a mid-level programmer at your company. What it does mean, though, is that, when used properly -- no vibe coding here -- AI could help clean up old but still used code; maintain abandoned programs; and improve existing code.Also: The overselling of AI - and how to resist itFor example, Dirk Hondhel, Verizon's senior director of open source, posted on LinkedIn that while AI coding tools aren't yet ready to maintain code, he believes they will be soon. "This is almost possible today. And at the rate of improvement these tools have seen over the last couple of quarters, I am convinced that it will be possible with acceptable results at some point this year."He's not the only one. Ruby project maintainer Stan Lo (st0012) wrote that AI has already helped him with documentation themes, refactors, and debugging, and he explicitly wonders whether AI tools will "help revive unmaintained projects" and "raise a new generation of contributors -- or even maintainers." Indeed, there's already one AI project, Autonomous Transpilation for Legacy Application Systems (ATLAS), that helps developers modernize legacy codebases for modern programming languages. We can expect to see other such AI tools appearing soon. There's a lot of obsolete but still-used code out there that could use a modern refresh. The lawyers are going to have a field day Before breaking out the champagne, let's consider several major problems. First, if we can improve open-source code with AI, what's to stop someone from copying and rewriting existing code and then putting it under a proprietary license? The lawyers are going to have a field day with this. Oh, wait! -- they soon will: Dan Blanchard, maintainer of an important Python library called chardet, just released the latest "clean room" version of the program under the MIT license, replacing its GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL). By "clean room," he means he used Anthropic's Claude to rewrite the library entirely. Claude is now listed as a project contributor. A person claiming to be the project's original developer, Mark Pilgrim, is not happy. Pilgrim says, "[The maintainers'] claim that it is a 'complete rewrite' is irrelevant, since they had ample exposure to the originally licensed code. Adding a fancy code generator into the mix does not somehow grant them any additional rights."Also: AI is getting scary good at finding hidden software bugs - even in decades-old codeBlanchard, however, claims that "chardet 7 is not derivative of earlier versions." Did I mention that using AI to modify or clone open-source code will end up in court? There's another problem: Although it appears that AI is much more useful than it used to be for fixing code issues, there's still a lot of AI slop out there, and open-source project maintainers are drowning in it. Just ask Daniel Stenberg, creator of the popular open-source data transfer program cURL. Pretty much every open-source project maintainer can tell the same story. In some cases, the AI slop has proven so poisonous that the project itself has died. For example, Python Software Foundation's Jannis Leidel, the lead maintainer of Jazzband, closed the program down because the "flood of AI-generated spam PRs and issues" drowned the project. Torvalds himself, a wary AI user, warns that while AI generates code quickly, the results can be "horrible to maintain." He views AI as a tool that boosts productivity, but it doesn't replace the need to actually understand what's going on in a program when things break. And, I assure you, things will break.Also: How Claude Code's new auto mode prevents AI coding disasters - without slowing you downThe Linux Foundation's security organizations, the Alpha-Omega Project and the Open Source Security Foundation (OpenSSF), are addressing this issue by making AI tools available to maintainers at no cost. Kroah-Hartman said of it, "OpenSSF has the active resources needed to support numerous projects that will help these overworked maintainers with the triage and processing of the increased AI-generated security reports they are currently receiving."While AI is becoming truly useful for open-source developers and maintainers, there are still a lot of legal, coding, and quality issues to address before AI and open-source programming will truly work together in harmony.

评论（默认中文）

MithrilTuxedo 2026/04/01 23:33
我想到了 Debian，以及它需要付出多少努力才能随着时间的推移保持稳定性和安全性。

我无法想象如果不使用人工智能来让我们看到它的可靠性，我们真的能够信任它。

I'm thinking of Debian and how much effort it takes to maintain stability and security over time.

I can't imagine we'll really be able to trust AI without it's use in open source software where we can see how reliable it is.

ghywertelling 2026/04/02 01:01
如果人工智能发挥作用，想象一下 Android 手机需要 10 年的安全更新，甚至可能需要 10 年的全面操作系统升级。
If AI works, imagine 10 years of security updates and possibly 10 years of full OS upgrades for Android phones.

why would they do that when they want to sell you a new phone?

With AI you can do that, or smaller companies can do that. It levels the field.

Hamuko 2026/04/02 00:05
当人们建议将人工智能用于开源项目时，考虑到开源项目预算中位数几乎为 0 美元/月，他们到底提倡什么？如果维护者想要为该项目建立一个网站，也许每月 1 美元。
When people suggest to use AI for open-source projects, what exactly are they advocating for given that the median open-source project budget is pretty much $0/month? Maybe $1/month if the maintainer likes to have a website for the project.

hparadiz 2026/04/02 01:11
每月 20 美元的订阅费用比我支付的电费还要少，而且本地型号也足够强大。许多顶级开源项目已经向开发人员支付了报酬。
The $20 / month subscription is less than I pay for electricity already and the local models are also capable enough. A lot of the top open source projects have paid devs working on them already.
在 HN 中查看
ivaivanova 2026/04/02 01:07
哈，我刚刚发布了一个使用 Anthropic API 的开源库。我解决成本问题的方法是让用户自带 API 密钥。我这边的基础设施成本为零，他们为他们使用的东西付费。
Ha, I just shipped an open source library using the Anthropic API. The way I solved the cost concern was by having users bring their own API key. Zero infra cost on my end and they pay for what they use.

s_ting765 2026/04/02 01:09
编码代理就像向精灵索要代码。他们会给你你要求的代码，但你永远不知道为你设置了什么样的诅咒。
Coding agents are like asking a genie for code. They will give you the code you ask for alright but you never know what kind of curse has been crontabbed for you.

kjkjadksj 2026/04/02 00:18
在移动设备上，您可以阅读一小段 1.5 英寸的条带。屏幕的其余部分是自动播放的广告。不，我阅读这篇文章时并没有经历这些。

klibertp 2026/04/02 01:07
我（从第二段）了解到，12 人中有 7 人是“绝大多数”。读完之后我有点不愿意继续读下去了……
I learned (from the second paragraph) that 7 out of 12 is "vast majority". I'm a bit reluctant to read on after that...
在 HN 中查看
hagbard_c 2026/04/02 02:35
> 屏幕的其余部分是自动播放广告。

仅当您在该设备上没有应有的可用内容拦截器时。使用没有内容拦截器的网络设备与在当地垃圾场日光浴一样对您的健康有害。只需安装一个内容拦截器即可保护自己免受污物的侵害。如果您碰巧使用的设备不允许有效的内容阻止 - 来自水果工厂的东西或完全粘性的东西 - 您可能需要考虑购买更符合以下内容的设备 你的 需求较少，而上述公司的需求较少。

> Rest of the screen is autoplaying ads.

Only if you don't have a working content blocker on that device which you should have. Using web devices without content blockers is just as bad for your health as sunbathing on the local garbage dump. Just get a content blocker and protect yourself from the filth. If you happen to use a device which does not allow effective content blocking - something from the fruit factory or something totally googly - you might want to consider getting something which is more aligned with your needs and less with those of the aforementioned companies.

supernes 2026/04/01 22:54

TLDR: Greg Kroah-Hartman says that last month something magical happened and AI output is no longer "slop".

throwaway2027 2026/04/02 00:09
我去年12月就注意到了。
I noticed it last December.
在 HN 中查看
BatFastard 2026/04/02 03:04
十二月对我来说也是如此，从产生很多幻觉，到表现得非常好。我猜这是作品 4.5？
December for me too, went from hallucinating a lot, to doing really well. I guess it was opus 4.5?
在 HN 中查看
no_shadowban_6 2026/04/02 00:51

paradigm shift, bro.
在 HN 中查看
dragonelite 2026/04/02 01:11
让我猜猜这项大型技术检查终于通过了？
Let me guess the big tech cheque finally cleared?
在 HN 中查看
georgemcbay 2026/04/01 23:28
IMO 去年 12 月左右，LLM 输出（至少对于编码，不是所有事情）从“几乎 100% 肯定会失败”变成“如果你在意识到上下文限制的同时要求正确的事情，那么可能不会失败”。

很多人似乎仍坚持他们较旧的（当时正确的）观点，但仍然总是产生废话。

FWIW，我更像是人工智能的终结者（从某种意义上说，考虑到我们的政治现实，我认为人工智能带来的经济结果对于知识工作者来说将是灾难性的），而不是支持者，但就完成工作的效用而言，他们最近确实通过了一个明显的拐点。

IMO around December of last year LLM output (for coding at least, not for everything) went from "almost 100% certainly slop" to "probably not slop, if you asked for the right thing while being aware of context limitations".

A lot of people seem stuck with their older (correct at the time) views of them still always producing slop.

FWIW I am more of an AI doomer (in the sense that I think the economic results from them will be disastrous for knowledge workers given our political realities) than booster, but in terms of utility to get work done they did pass a clear inflection point quite recently.

在 HN 中查看
bluefirebrand 2026/04/01 23:32
> 如果您在意识到上下文限制的同时要求正确的事情

因此，在大多数情况下仍然很可能产生溢出

如果它们最有用的地方是你已经将事情指定到那么精确的地方，那么它们就没那么有用了吗？

毕竟，如此精确地指定事物是一项耗时且困难的工作。

> if you asked for the right thing while being aware of context limitations

So, still pretty likely to produce slop in a large majority of cases

If the most useful place for them is where you've already specced things out to that degree of precision then they aren't that useful?

Speccing things to that precision is the time consuming and difficult work anyways, after all.

在 HN 中查看
georgemcbay 2026/04/01 23:39
我认为法学硕士目前需要由那些知道自己在做什么来创造价值的人使用，但它们从无休止的垃圾机器到正确手中的有用工具的飞跃足以让我认为，它们将成为即使是未经培训的大众手中的有用工具只是时间问题。

我希望这不是真的，因为我认为这会在经济上颠覆我所从事的行业，但可悲的是，宇宙并不关心我的愿望。

I think LLMs currently need to be used by someone who knows what they are doing to produce value, but the jump they made from being endless slop machines to useful tools in the right hands is enough for me to assume it is only a matter of time until they will be useful tools in the hands of even the untrained masses.

I wish this wasn't true because I think it will economically upend the industry in which I have a career, but sadly the universe doesn't care what I wish.

在 HN 中查看
mjr00 2026/04/01 23:58
> 假设它们迟早会成为未经训练的大众手中的有用工具。

在我看来，这大大高估了“未经训练的大众”以逻辑、数学方式思考的能力。显然，像微积分 II 这样的基础知识在大多数大学中的失败率约为 50%。

> assume it is only a matter of time until they will be useful tools in the hands of even the untrained masses.

IMO this vastly overestimates how good the "untrained masses" are at thinking in a logical, mathematical way. Apparently something as basic as Calculus II has a fail rate of ~50% in most universities.

在 HN 中查看
chromacity 2026/04/02 01:24
这是怎么回事？

微积分 II 没有任何“基础”内容。微积分在数学教育中受到独特的诅咒，因为它之前的一切都或多或少植根于对现实世界的直觉，而微积分则建立在更加抽象的公理之上 和 没有得到很好的证实（直到你的数学教育后期）。我预计许多聪明、足智多谋的人都会失败，而且我认为这更多地说明了我们正在教授的抽象概念。

而且，促使法学硕士取得好的成绩远没有微积分那么复杂。

How does this follow?

There's nothing "basic" about Calculus II. Calculus is uniquely cursed in mathematical education because everything that comes before it is more or less rooted in intuition about the real world, while calculus is built on axioms that are far more abstract and not substantiated well (not until later in your mathematical education). I expect many intelligent, resourceful people to fail it and I think it says more about the abstractions we're teaching than anything else.

But also, prompting LLMs to give good results is nowhere near as complex as calculus.

在 HN 中查看
isueej 2026/04/02 00:40
这就是为什么你不能在这里概括意见。

这里的大多数人都不属于那群人。因此，ofc 他们可以找到一种方法，从需要一些修补和玩耍的事物中创造价值。

问题是这些技术能否发展成为以最小的努力生产产品的技术——同时只知道最低限度的知识。我个人并不相信——这是一个白日梦，忽视了生产东西所必需的与生俱来的技能。

That’s why you can’t generalise opinions on here.

Most people on here don’t belong to that group of people. So ofc they can find a way to create value out of a thing that requires some tinkering and playing with.

The question is can the techniques evolve to become technologies to produce stuff with minimal effort - whilst only knowing the bare minimum. I’m not convinced personally - it’s a pipe dream and overlooks the innate skill necessary to produce stuff.

在 HN 中查看
xyzelement 2026/04/02 00:20
谁在乎？人们知道自己想要什么和需要什么，而人工智能越来越能够从中获取它们。
Who cares? People know what they want and need and AI is increasingly able to take it from there.
在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/04/02 00:37
> 人们知道自己想要什么和需要什么

如果他们真的这样做了，就不会有大量的人的角色基本上是“获取用户/管理人员所说的他们想要的东西，找出他们真正想要的东西，然后为其他人写下来”。

也许我为太多的初创公司工作过，只为大公司提供咨询服务，但在企业的任何地方，我都看到很多问题，基本上都是“其他人误解了那个人的意思”和/或“有人认为他们想要 X，他们实际上想要 Y”。

> People know what they want and need

If they truly did, there wouldn't be a huge amount of humans whose role is basically "Take what users/executives say they want, and figure out what they REALLY want, then write that down for others".

Maybe I've worked for too many startups, and only consulted for larger companies, but everywhere in businesses I see so many problems that are basically "Others misunderstood what that person meant" and/or "Someone thought they wanted X, they actually wanted Y".

在 HN 中查看
mjr00 2026/04/02 00:42
> 人们知道自己想要什么和需要什么

“业务分析师”和“产品负责人”（有时还有“客户成功”）等角色的存在数十年，有力地证明情况并非如此。

> People know what they want and need

The multi-decade existence of roles like "business analysts" and "product owners" (and sometimes "customer success") is pretty strong evidence that this is not the case.

在 HN 中查看
PhilipRoman 2026/04/02 00:39
他们想要什么？有时。他们需要什么？几乎从来没有。
What they want? Sometimes. What they need? Almost never.

在 HN 中查看
bluefirebrand 2026/04/02 00:44
> 我希望这不是真的，因为我认为这会在经济上颠覆我所从事的行业，但遗憾的是宇宙并不关心我的愿望。

我的意思是，是的。我也担心我的职业生涯，但出于不同的原因。我不认为这些东西实际上足以取代我，但我确实认为这对于签署支票的人来说并不重要。

我不相信法学硕士能产出比废液更好的东西。我认为人们的标准长期以来一直在下降，并将继续下降，直到达到法学硕士所达到的水平

问题不是 只是 法学硕士以及他们产生废液的事实，人们总体上对废液相当满意

但我不是，所以大多数软件行业已经没有我的位置了

> I wish this wasn't true because I think it will economically upend the industry in which I have a career, but sadly the universe doesn't care what I wish.

I mean, yes. I'm worried about my career too, but for different reasons. I don't think these things are actually good enough to replace me, but I do think it doesn't matter to the people signing the cheques.

I don't believe LLMs are producing anything better than slop. I think people's standards have been sinking for a long time and will continue to sink until they reach the level LLMs produce

The problem isn't just LLMs and the fact they produce slop, it's that people are overall pretty fine with slop

I'm not though, so there's no place for me in most software business anymore

在 HN 中查看
isueej 2026/04/02 01:06
我不是瑞典人。

但我从软件作为对象的角度来看待它们。

因为它是无形的，人们无法看到其内部。所以有些东西看起来很漂亮，即使它的底层是邋遢的。

然而，存在一个隐含的权衡——从安全角度来看，安装倾斜会使您更容易受到攻击，错误等可能会破坏使用该软件的信任和体验。这基本上会使企业的生命面临风险。

人们并没有过多考虑这种风险——因为这种风险还没有发生在任何人身上。我的想法是，斜坡会继续不受控制地安装吗？或者人们是否期望进行改进，使自己能够使用更强大的工具回去清理污水？

如果后者没有发生，我认为我们将看到更多的公司面临压力。

但总的来说，我认为太多的注意力集中在产出的加速上。我从来不认为这是最重要的事情。拥有清晰的视力是次要的。问题是，拥有清晰的愿景需要做大量繁重的工作——它训练和调节你的思维以一种特定的方式思考。

看看这一切如何展开将会很有趣。

I’m not a SWE.

But I look at software from the perspective of them as being objects.

Since it’s intangible people can’t see within. So something can look pretty even if underlying it all, it’s slop.

However, there is an implicit trade off - mounting slop makes you more vulnerable from a security standpoint, bugs etc which can destroy trust and experience of using the software. This can essentially put the life of a business at risk.

People aren’t thinking so much about that risk - because it hasn’t happened to anyone large substantially. What I think about is will slop just continue to mount unchecked? Or are people expecting there to be improvements that enable oneself to go back and clean up the slop with more powerful tooling?

If the latter does not come about, I think we will see more firms come under stress.

Overall though, I think too much focus is on the acceleration of output. I never think that’s the most important thing. It’s secondary to having a crystal clear vision. The problem is to have a clear vision requires doing a lot of grunt work - it trains and conditions your mind to think a particular kind of way.

It will be interesting to see how this all plays out.

在 HN 中查看
abletonlive 2026/04/02 01:22 deleted
[已标记]
[flagged]
在 HN 中查看
saltcured 2026/04/02 02:06
根据你最后几句话，我很确定你会解雇我。但是，我有一个零假设需要考虑......

正如您所暗示的那样，我认为个人门槛的跨越会给人一种错误的印象，即本月或上个月或去年“一切都改变了”。就像你说的，在某个特定的月份里发生的主要变化是观察者。

但是，也许人工智能的顿悟并没有意识到人工智能已经有多好了。相反，可能是当个人的标准下降时，相同的人工智能使用被视为一种好处而不是一种负担。这两种解释都产生了我们现在看到的相同的采用和评论的基本模式。

差异将在于长期结果。几年后，我们是否会看到这种大规模采用带来了生产力和质量的复兴，或者是因溢出引起的责任和损失的灾难？

Based on your last sentences, I am pretty sure you will dismiss me. But, I have a null hypothesis to consider...

Like you implied, I think a personal threshold crossing gives this false impression that "everything changed" this month or last month or last year. Like you said, the main thing that changed in one particular month was the observer.

But, perhaps the AI epiphany is not waking up to recognize how good AI already was. Instead, it could be when an individual's standards degrade such that the same AI usage is seen as a benefit instead of a liability. Both interpretations yield the same basic pattern of adoption and commentary that we see right now.

The difference will be in the long-term outcome. Some years from now, will we see that this mass adoption yielded a renaissance of productivity and quality, or a cataclysm of slop-induced liability and loss?

在 HN 中查看
keeda 2026/04/02 03:27
我知道诉诸权威可能是一个谬论，但诉诸多数同意的权威是有道理的。多位以其技术精湛而闻名的知名人士一直在支持人工智能辅助编码，因此很难说他们每个人都降低了自己的标准。

看到反LLM技术圈子里的认知失调是很有趣的，因为他们崇拜的技术巨头，从托瓦尔兹到卡马克一直到高德纳，对人工智能说一些积极的话，更不用说赞扬它了！

I know appeal to authority can be a fallacy, but there is something to be said for appeal to a preponderence of concurring authorities. Multiple notable personalities known for their technical chops have been endorsing AI assisted coding, so it's hard to argue that every one of them lowered their standards.

It's been fun seeing the cognitive dissonance in anti-LLM tech circles as technical giants that they idolized, from Torvalds through Carmack all the way up to Knuth, say something positive about AI, let alone sing praises of it!

在 HN 中查看
jason_oster 2026/04/02 02:55
不得不指出，“个人标准过高”本身就是致命的缺陷。我见过的质量最差的代码来自缺乏自我意识或谦逊的开发人员。他们称自己为工匠，对他们留下的错误和安全漏洞的雷区不承担任何责任。互联网与泡泡糖和打包线结合在一起 [1] [2] 因为工匠拒绝自我改进。

这些工匠抱怨人工智能生成的代码有多糟糕。人工智能是 接受过你糟糕的工匠代码的训练。就像他们第一次照镜子，并对自己所看到的感到厌恶。

[1]： https://techcrunch.com/2014/03/29/the-internet-is-held-toget...

[2]： https://krebsonsecurity.com/2021/11/the-internet-is-held-tog...

I have to point out that having "high personal standards" is its own fatal flaw. The worst quality code I've seen comes from developers with little self awareness or humility. They call themselves artisans and take no responsibility for the minefield of bugs and security vulnerabilities left in their wake. The Internet is held together with bubblegum and baling wire [1] [2] because artisans reject self improvement.

These same artisans complain about how bad AI generated code is. The AI is trained on your bad artisan code. It's like they are looking in the mirror for the first time and being disgusted by what they see.

[1]: https://techcrunch.com/2014/03/29/the-internet-is-held-toget...

[2]: https://krebsonsecurity.com/2021/11/the-internet-is-held-tog...

在 HN 中查看
ramesh31 2026/04/01 23:39
>TLDR：Greg Kroah-Hartman 说上个月发生了一些神奇的事情，AI 输出不再“马虎”。

Opus 4.6 发生了重大变化。简直就再也不会错了。您可能需要继续进一步澄清什么 你 想要的，但它现在想做的事情永远不会犯错误。

>TLDR: Greg Kroah-Hartman says that last month something magical happened and AI output is no longer "slop".

Opus 4.6 has been a step change. It's simply never wrong anymore. You may need to continue giving it further clarification as to what you want, but it never makes mistakes with what it intends to do now.

在 HN 中查看
Balinares 2026/04/02 00:30
哟，仅仅因为你无法判断克劳德什么时候错了，并不意味着它是对的。

我确实同意 2026 年第一季度的型号总体上已经过了一个门槛，但天哪，Opus 4.6 仍然搞砸了很多。

Yo, just because you can't tell when Claude is wrong, doesn't mean it's right.

I do agree that the Q1 2026 models in general have passed a threshold, but goodness almighty Opus 4.6 still screws up a lot.

binarymax 2026/04/02 00:21

taormina 2026/04/02 01:03

在 HN 中查看
pbiggar 2026/04/02 00:15
> 发生了什么？ Kroah-Hartman 耸耸肩：“我们不知道。似乎没有人知道为什么。要么更多的工具变得更好，要么人们开始......”

奇怪的情绪。很明显，这些工具去年（我记得是四月份）跨越了一个门槛，它们变得足够好，可以实际编写整个应用程序，并且从那里开始加速。如今，它们非常棒，据我所知，没有人再编写手工代码了（至少在工作中）。

> What happened? Kroah-Hartman shrugged: "We don't know. Nobody seems to know why. Either a lot more tools got a lot better, or people started going ..."

Odd sentiment. It's pretty clear the tools crossed a threshold last year (in April as I recall) where they became good enough to actually write entire applications, and just accelerated from there. Today they're amazing and no-one I know is writing artisanal code anymore (at least, not at work).

在 HN 中查看
neonstatic 2026/04/02 01:47
> 我们不知道。似乎没有人知道为什么

这是埋藏的lede。这是一个宣传片。

> We don't know. Nobody seems to know why

This is the buried lede. It's a propaganda piece.
